text: Writing clean code is essential for any programmer. Readable code makes it easier for others—and your future self—to understand what the code does. A good practice is to follow naming conventions: use clear, descriptive names for variables, functions, and classes. For example, instead of `x`, use `userAge`. Keep your functions short and focused on a single task. This not only simplifies testing but also improves maintainability. When each function has one purpose, updating or fixing bugs becomes much simpler. Ultimately, writing clean code saves time and reduces errors, making it a valuable skill for long-term projects.
